entry.title: Re: Change User ID in SAP Cloud Identity Services
entry.content:
Hi @LX_ ,

my problem is, that we connected different systems with our test and production IAS in the past.

For that purpose, we always created users in the tenants as needed.

So now we have a completely different user base in test and production tenant.

Now with the implementation of Cloud ERP it might lead to the case that we need the same users in the two tenants with same user ids (e.g. when working with workflows in Cloud ERP) and I have no idea how I should handle that...

BR Tobi

entry.title: Re: Sub contracting
entry.content:
Practical mapping options

ERP Subcontracting Process:
Use subcontracting POs for external operations, with material issue and receipt flows.

Production Orders:
For internal operations, use production orders with goods issue and receipt postings.

Batch Determination:
Configure batch determination so that the correct batch flows through each operation.

Reporting:
Use batch-wise stock reports (e.g., MB52 in SAP or equivalent) to show stock at each stage.

The key decision is whether you want one continuous batch number across all four operations (strong traceability) or different batch numbers per stage (clearer stock separation). If your client’s priority is visibility at each stage, separate material codes with batch tracking is usually cleaner.

entry.title: SAP EWM Storage Control Deep Dive: Combining Process-Oriented and Layout-Oriented Strategies
entry.content:
SAP EWM Storage Control Deep Dive: Combining Process-Oriented and Layout-Oriented Strategies

Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

1.Abstract:

This use case considers a scenario wherein both storage control processes are required at same time. In this example, two processes (Unloading + Put away) and before final put away it should through Intermediate bin.

Here Process oriented storage control is having

 #1 Unloading

#2 Put away

However, during #2, Layout oriented storage control triggers depending upon the final storage bin and move the material to Intermediate bin and the complete the put away process.

                                                                                                                           

 

2.0 Introduction:

One of the important feature of SAP EWM is Storage control process.  It is mechanism which dictate how material will move inside the warehouse with how many steps and what would be the sequence of those steps. It says stage like sequence of movement and other steps like staging, deconsolidation, value addition services and finally physical Movement among the storage types. There are two storage type control:

  1. Process oriented storage control(POSC)

This control the logical process steps like Unloading, Deconsolidation, Labelling, Quality inspection, Packing, Unpacking, staging, put away etc.

  1. Layout oriented storage control(LOSC)

This control the physical movement of material inside the warehouse, like staging area, Conveyor routing, Intermediate bins etc.

3.0 Detailed process:

As per the business requirement, we can use POSC and LOSC separately or can even make hybrid approach, which combine POSC and LOSC both.

In this use case, we are using the hybrid approach POSC + LOSC for Inbound process. POSC is having two steps Unload and Put away, however during Put away, before final put away, need to move the material at intermediate bin and finally complete the put away.

#1 Door Determination:

Configuration and master data settings are as follows:

  1. Configuration

 

 

 

 

  1. b) Master data

b1) Created below bins as staging area under storage type 9010

 

b2) Created below intermediate bin under intermediate storage type 0082

 

b3) Setting Warehouse process type along with staging area group, bay and warehouse door.

 

#2 POSC and LOSC:

  1. Configuration

 

Final storage type for raw material

 

 

Intermediate Storage type:

 

Warehouse process type:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

POSC:

 

 

 

LOSC configuration:

 

Testing:

#1) Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1):

 

 

#2 Inbound delivery(VL31N):

 

 

 

#3 Inbound delivery distributed in EWM(/SCWM/PRDI):

 

 

Step1: Below WT/WO is created(/SCWM/MON): Confirmed WT # 1712 in background, it means unloading process is completed

#1As pruduct master is having process determination as T8, warehouse process WP10 determined.

#2 Final storage type ST05 is determined, based on storage search configuration.

#3 As Product master is having door determination D001, it will determined the goods movement bin.

 

 

 

Step2: AS soon as unloading process is completed and destination storage type is ST15, so LOSC triggered and moved the stock to Intermediate bin 0821-IN-BIN

 

Step3: Finally, material will be moved from Intermediate bin 0821-IN-BIN to final bin ST15-05

 

 

 

 

 

4.Conclusion:

This scenario is applied when POSC and LOSC needs to be executed combinedly. The combined use of POSC and LOSC in SAP EWM enables organizations to accurately model both process-driven steps and physical warehouse movements within a single integrated framework.

The configuration, master data, and transactional flow outlined in this document provide a good reference for fellow SAP consultants to design, map, and implement robust storage control scenarios in SAP EWM.

entry.title: Intracompany STO with inbound ASR in Embedded EWM –Automatic IBD Creation at PGI With FU Reassign.
entry.content:
Overview 

This blog describes an intracompany stock transport order (STO) scenario using Advanced shipping and receiving (ASR) for inbound execution with embedded SAP EWM. 

A key enhancement in this scenario is the automatic creation of the inbound delivery at post goods issue (PGI) of the outbound delivery, triggered via SPED output, along with automatic reassignment of the same Freight Unit (FU) from outbound to inbound delivery. 

The solution supports end–to-end execution across SAP S/4 HANA, SAP TM, SAP EWM, and ASR, ensuring uninterrupted transportation and warehouse execution using a single Freight Unit and Freight Order. 

Business Scope and Supported Scenarios 

This functionality applies to intracompany stock transfers and supports inbound ASR execution when outbound processing is performed from either EWM-managed or non-EWM storage locations. 

Supported Combinations 

  • Outbound ASR shipping point+EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR shipping point+ EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound ASR shipping point+EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+non-EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound ASR shipping point+ NON EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound non-ASR +NON EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+EWM Storage location 

 This flexibility allows organizations to run Hybrid Landscapes without breaking ASR or transportation execution. 

Key Features

  • Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) enabled for inbound STO processing 
  • Embedded EWM support for inbound warehouse execution 
  • Outbound execution from EWM or non-EWM storage locations 
  • Automatic inbound delivery creation at PGI using SPED output 
  • Automatic reassignment of the same Freight Unit from outbound to inbound delivery 
  • Continuous TM execution using one Freight Unit and one Freight order 
  • Updated document flow in the TM Freight Order after reassignment 

End-to-End Process Flow 

1.Transportation and Delivery Preparation

  • Stock Transport Order (STO) is created with EWM relevance. ￼

 

  • Freight Unit (FU) is created in SAPTM. 

 

           

 

  • Plan Freight Unit into FO using TM Optimizer, Automatic carrier selection is performed. 

 

          

 

         

 

  • Create Outbound Delivery (OBD) with FO.

 

          

 

  • Delivery proposal is generated and distributed to SAPEWM 

     

2.Outbound Warehouse Execution (Source Warehouse)

  • Picking and packing are executed in SAP EWM.
  •  

  •  

     

  • ASR execution at the source warehouse: 
  • Check-in 
  • Door check-in 
  • Loading of packages 
  • Loading end  
  • Delivery split is performed, if required 
  • ASR cockpit checkout and PGI (Post Goods Issue) are executed.
  •  
  •  

     

3.SPED Output and Inbound Delivery Creation (New Feature), inbound warehouse execution 

  • SPED output is triggered automatically at PGI of the outbound delivery.
  •  

           

 

  • Inbound delivery (IBD) is created automatically
  •  
  •  

     

  • Freight Unit reassignment occurs automatically:
  • The same FU used for outbound execution is reassigned to the inbound delivery 
  • No new FU created 
  •  

  •  

  • This step is the core enhancement and ensures FU continuity across outbound and inbound execution. 
  • ASR at destination warehouse 
  • Check-in 
  • Door check-in 
  • Unloading of packages 
  • Unloading end  
  • Post Goods Receipt (PGR) for inbound delivery
  •  
  •  

     

  • ASR check out
  • Put Away to destination bin in EWM
  •  

  •  

Benefits 

  • Increased flexibility for ASR processes- supports multiple combinations of EWM and non-EWM storage locations. 
  • Improved Intracompany Stock Movement- Ensures a smooth, integrated STO execution across outbound and inbound logistics. 
  • Reduced Manul Effort- Automation of inbound delivery creation and FU reassignment eliminates manual steps. 
  • Increased Data Accuracy- single FU and FO ensures consistent transportation and warehouse data 

Conclusion

  • The enhanced intracompany STO with inbound ASR in embedded EWM delivers a seamless, end-to-end logistics process by tightly integrating SAP TM, SAP EWM and ASR. 
  • The automatic creation of inbound delivery at PGI via SPED output, combined with Freight unit reassignment, ensures continuous transportation visibility, eliminates document fragmentation, and significantly reduces operational effort. 
  • For organizations operating hybrid EWM and non-EWM landscapes, this functionality provides execution flexibility, better data consistency, and a simplified logistics process.

entry.title: The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated OPR
entry.content:
The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated Operations

 

A White Paper by Atul Joshi

 

Executive Summary

Utilities today face a paradox: they must modernize rapidly to support digital customer expectations, DER integration, and grid intelligence — yet they operate in one of the most regulated, risk‑averse environments in the world. Traditional SAP landscapes, especially SAP IS‑U, are burdened with decades of custom code, point‑to‑point integrations, and rigid processes that make transformation slow, expensive, and operationally risky.

This white paper introduces The Utility Clean Core Blueprint, a modernization model that enables utilities to evolve their SAP landscape without destabilizing missions, critical billing, metering, and customer operations. The blueprint leverages SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as the innovation and integration layer, allowing utilities to decouple custom logic, orchestrate processes, and adopt cloud capabilities — all while keeping the SAP core stable, compliant, and upgrade‑ready.

 

  1. The Modernization Challenge in Regulated Utilities

 

Utilities operate under unique constraints:

  • Regulatory oversight limits downtime and process changes.
  • High‑volume billing cycles require predictable system performance.
  • Legacy customizations make upgrades risky and expensive.
  • Aging IS‑U systems cannot support modern digital experiences.
  • Integration sprawl slows innovation and increases operational risk.

Most utilities want to move toward S/4HANA Utilities, but the path is blocked by:

  • 10–20 years of Z‑code
  • tightly coupled interfaces
  • custom workflows embedded in IS‑U
  • inflexible monolithic architecture

This is where Clean Core becomes not just a best practice — but a survival strategy.

2. What Clean Core Really Means for Utilities

 

Clean Core is often misunderstood as “removing custom code.” For utilities, it means something deeper:

Clean Core = A stable SAP transactional engine + an agile innovation layer on SAP BTP

This approach allows:

  • predictable billing and metering operations
  • faster innovation cycles
  • safer upgrades
  • cloud‑ready architecture
  • regulatory compliance

In other words: Keep the core clean. Move the complexity out. Modernize without breaking operations.

3. SAP BTP: The Enabler of Utility Modernization

 

SAP BTP provides the capabilities utilities need to modernize safely:

 

Key BTP Services for Utilities

Capability

BTP Service

Utility Value

Event-driven decoupling

Event Mesh

Real-time meter events, outage events, billing triggers

Integration modernization

Integration Suite

Replace PI/PO, eliminate point-to-point interfaces

Extension development

SAP CAP / Kyma

Move Z‑logic out of IS‑U

Workflow automation

BTP Workflow

Field service approvals, credit workflows

Data intelligence

HANA Cloud

Meter analytics, billing insights

Security & governance

IAS/IPS

Unified identity for customer and employee apps

BTP becomes the innovation shell around the SAP core.

 

 

4. The Utility Clean Core Blueprint

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint consists of five practical steps:

Step 1 — Stabilize the Core

  • Freeze custom development in IS‑U
  • Identify Z‑objects that block upgrades
  • Classify custom code into: retire, refactor, or externalize
  • Clean up unused interfaces

Step 2 — Externalize Custom Logic to BTP

 

Move the following out of IS‑U:

  • complex billing rules
  • validation logic
  • workflow approvals
  • customer-facing processes
  • meter data transformations

Use CAP, Node.js, or Java on BTP.

Step 3 — Modernize Integrations

Replace:

  • PI/PO mappings
  • RFC-based interfaces
  • batch jobs

With:

  • Event Mesh
  • Integration Suite
  • APIs

Step 4 — Introduce Event-Driven Architecture

Trigger events for:

  • meter reads
  • billing completion
  • move-in/move-out
  • credit actions
  • outage notifications

This decouples processes and reduces core load.

Step 5 — Enable Coexistence with S/4HANA

Run IS‑U + S/4HANA + BTP in hybrid mode:

  • IS‑U remains the billing engine
  • S/4 handles finance, procurement, asset management
  • BTP orchestrates innovation

This avoids a risky “big bang” migration.

5. Practical Utility Case Studies (Realistic, Practitioner-Level)

Case Study 1: Reducing Billing Run Failures with BTP Event Mesh

A North American utility struggled with billing run failures caused by custom validations embedded in IS‑U.

Problem: Every enhancement point slowed down billing and caused unpredictable dumps. Solution:

  • Move validation logic to a CAP service on BTP
  • Trigger validation via Event Mesh before billing
  • Return results via API

Outcome:

  • Billing runtime reduced by 27%
  • Zero dumps in 3 months
  • Clean Core achieved without touching core billing logic

Case Study 2: Modernizing Move-In/Move-Out Without Touching IS‑U

 

A regulated utility needed a digital customer onboarding experience.

Problem: IS‑U move-in/out processes were too rigid and heavily customized.

Solution:

  • Build a customer onboarding app on BTP
  • Orchestrate workflow using BTP Workflow
  • Trigger IS‑U move-in via API only at the final step

Outcome:

  • Customer onboarding time reduced from 3 days to 30 minutes
  • No changes required in IS‑U core
  • Regulatory compliance maintained

 

Case Study 3: Eliminating 40+ Point-to-Point Integrations

Problem: European utility had PI/PO interfaces that were expensive to maintain.

Solution:

  • Replace PI/PO with Integration Suite
  • Introduce canonical data models
  • Use Event Mesh for asynchronous processes

Outcome:

  • Integration failures reduced by 60%
  • Upgrade downtime reduced by 40%
  • IT operations cost reduced significantly

 

6. The Coexistence Architecture: IS‑U + S/4 + BTP

Utilities cannot afford a big-bang migration. The coexistence model allows:

IS‑U

→ Billing, metering, device management

S/4HANA

→ Finance, procurement, asset management

SAP BTP

→ Innovation, integration, automation, analytics

This architecture supports:

  • gradual modernization
  • regulatory stability
  • continuous innovation

7. Governance: The Missing Piece in Most Utility Programs

Clean Core is not a technical exercise — it is a governance discipline.

Key governance principles

  • No custom code in the core
  • All new extensions must be BTP-first
  • Event-driven architecture preferred over batch
  • API-led integration
  • Quarterly review of Z‑objects
  • Architecture board approval for all enhancements

This is how utilities stay modern after the transformation.

8. Conclusion: Modernize Without Disruption

Utilities cannot pause operations to modernize. They need a blueprint that:

  • protects regulated processes
  • reduces technical debt
  • accelerates innovation
  • prepares for S/4HANA
  • supports the energy transition

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint provides exactly that.

 

By using SAP BTP as the innovation layer, utilities can modernize safely, incrementally, and intelligently — without destabilizing the mission-critical systems that keep the lights on.

entry.title: Re: What is the difference between Actual costing and mat...
entry.content:
CK11N (Material Cost Estimate) is used for planned/standard costing before production, while CKMLCP (Actual Costing/Material Ledger) is used for actual costing at period-end to revalue materials based on real costs. They serve different purposes and coexist without conflict: CK11N sets the baseline, CKMLCP adjusts to reality.

Below are the comparison between the standard two transactions

  • Without CK11N, you lose planned cost visibility, making variance analysis impossible.
  • Without CKMLCP, Inventory remains valued at standard cost, which may not reflect reality, leading to inaccurate financials.
  • In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, some advanced costing functions may be simplified compared to on-premise, but the principle remains the same;  CK11N for planned, CKMLCP for actual

entry.title: Re: Cumulative Calculation Across Blended Models in SAP A...
entry.content:
@Preeti_G1 

You could calculate cumulative values in one of the model using Advance formula leveraging LINK syntax to lookup values from other models. Other option would be to converge the relevant data into one of the models using cross model data copy and then finally calculate cumulative using the story option or Data Action Advanced formula. Blending at story level has its own limitation as you have already highlighted in your post.

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: What’s New in Utilities: Highlights from the SAP Utilities Newsletter – April 2026
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

The SAP Utilities Newsletter - April 2026 is out, bringing together the most relevant updates for the energy and utilities community, from innovation highlights and AI insights to customer success stories, product updates, and upcoming events.

 

 

This month, we spotlight the following key topics, along with several additional updates and insights included in the full edition:

SAP for Energy & Utilities Conference
Preparations for our annual conference in Toulouse (April 21–23) are well underway. This edition offers a preview of a compelling agenda featuring inspiring keynotes, expert-led sessions, hands-on workshops, valuable industry perspectives, and dedicated networking opportunities. We look forward to welcoming the global utilities community to connect, learn, and exchange ideas.

SAP Distributed Energy Resources (DER) Enables the Energy Flexibility Market
An in-depth article explores how SAP DER, built on the SAP Business Technology Platform, unifies technical and commercial data, allowing utilities to coordinate millions of energy devices, enable energy sharing, support flexible pricing, and accelerate the transition to renewable energy.

SAP Utilities Innovation Update: Insights, Demos, and Roadmap
Discover the latest innovation updates across SAP Utilities solutions, including practical demos and roadmap perspectives. This section highlights how SAP is advancing SAP S/4HANA Utilities, customer experience, and asset-intensive processes to support utilities on their transformation journeys.

 
Celebrating Innovation in Utilities at the SAP Innovation Awards
This month’s edition also shines a spotlight on the SAP Innovation Awards, recognizing utilities that are pushing boundaries and delivering impact with SAP technology. These inspiring examples demonstrate how innovation, sustainability, and digital transformation come together in real-world projects.

GASAG: Digitalizing and Streamlining Critical Processes
GASAG is transforming its core operations by modernizing the IT landscape and migrating to SAP Utilities Core using RISE with SAP. The strategic move sets new industry standards for transparency, security, and future-ready innovation. The result: cloud-enabled billing operations, stronger regulatory compliance, and a scalable foundation for AI- driven process optimization.

AI Insights from Oxford Economics
A new Oxford Economics white paper, created in collaboration with SAP, explores how Energy and Utilities companies are leveraging artificial intelligence to strengthen decision-making, boost efficiency, and improve customer engagement. As the industry navigates increasing regulatory pressure, operational complexity, and rising expectations for transparency, AI is emerging as a critical enabler of resilience and responsiveness across the value chain.

SAP Business AI in Utilities Partner Live Expert Session 
Calling all partners! Join one of our 90-minute sessions on April 13 to learn how SAP Business AI helps Utilities work smarter, faster and more productively. Gain industry insights, explore real use cases, and learn how to position and sell AI to support your customers’ transformation.

 

Get It Straight to Your Inbox

To make sure you don’t miss future edition, you have the possibility to  👉 Subscribe to SAP Newsletters

Prefer to Read It Online?

No problem. You can access the full April 2026 edition here: 👉 SAP Utilities Newsletter – April 2026 (Online Edition)

Looking forward to meeting some of you in Toulouse!

entry.title: Clarification on Downpayment Payment Workflow Behavior in SAP
entry.content:
I would like to understand how to cancel parked documents created in the *Verify Supplier Down Payment Requests, particularly when the verification has been rejected.

Unfortunately, the flow described here does not provide any information on handling parked documents:Approval Workflows for Supplier Down Payment Requests | SAP Help Portal

I also attempted to use the *Display Parked Document, but received the error message:'Document 2210/15000000XX/2025 was created using Fiori applications; no processing.' The message suggests using the *Verify General Journal Entry, but the document cannot be selected there.

Is there a way to cancel a parked document for a supplier down payment request?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Downpayment Payment Workflow Behavio...
entry.content:
The parked documents are not posted yet, thus there is no need to reverse them.

And we do not suggest to delete the parked documents as they already have drawn the document numbers which are in sequence. To ensure there are no missing document numbers, we do not provide the deletion functionality with the Verify Supplier Down Payment Requests

If you want to delete it, this is a new requirement to be addressed. 

SAP Continuous Influence enables you to continuously suggest improvements to SAP Cloud solutions and our most recent technologies directly to the development teams.

entry.title: SAP Integrated Business Planning: Key 2025 Highlights and What’s Next
entry.content:
If the last few years have taught us anything, it’s that supply chains don’t break gently, they snap loudly, suddenly, and usually at the least convenient moment. One unexpected weather event, a policy shift, a clogged canal, or a supplier shutdown halfway across the world can ripple through your entire network before lunch.  

SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) is built exactly for this reality. It brings together AI-driven intelligence, real-time visibility, and connected processes so you can turn uncertainty into a strategic advantage and run planning with the same precision and resilience your customers expect. 

 

Innovations That Solve Real Planning Challenges 

SAP IBP accelerated its AI journey in 2025, weaving intelligent automation deep into planning workflows so you can spend less time fixing issues and more time shaping strategy. 

Joule AI now gives planners clearer, more explainable recommendations across demand, inventory, and supply optimization. Instead of wrestling with opaque algorithms, you get grounded insights that show why the system proposes a certain action, reinforcing trust and eliminating guesswork. 

The new Excel add-in takes this a step further by bringing planning intelligence directly into your daily workspace, helping you shorten routine tasks and boost productivity without changing how you work. 

On the data side, the Harmonized Planning Area (HPA) introduced in the 2508 release provides a unified backbone for end-to-end planning, closing the gaps between silos that have historically slowed down planners. Combined with modernized Planning UIs, SAP IBP reduces complexity and elevates user experience with automation that simplifies even the most intricate planning scenarios. 

 

Proven Impact Backed by Your Peers 

Over 30,000 active users across 79 countries and 24 industries log into SAP IBP daily, running 20,000 forecast algorithms and managing 8.1 trillion planning points in 2025 alone, that's enough data to stack paper halfway around the globe.  

Business leaders across industries are recognising measurable value, reflected in the consistently earns high marks on Gartner Peer Insights, TrustRadius, and G2. 

From usability to scalability to the strength of its AI-driven planning, customers across industries describe SAP IBP as a platform that finally matches the pace and complexity of modern supply chains. These ratings don’t just validate the technology; they validate the outcomes teams achieve with it. 

This community isn’t just using SAP IBP, they’re succeeding with it. With strong peer ratings on leading review platforms and performance at massive scale, SAP IBP has become a trusted foundation for organizations determined to modernize planning and stay resilient. 

 

Where the Real Value Shows Up: Resilient, Intelligent Planning 

It’s worth grounding the conversation in something more tangible: the real‑world impact planners create every single day. And when you zoom out to see what SAP IBP customers actually move, make, and deliver across industries, the scale is staggering, and the value becomes impossible to miss. 

Here’s what that looks like in numbers: 

  • Over 30 billion gallons of drinks planned, which is enough to fill 50,000 Olympic swimming pools. From soft drinks and juices to beer, wine, and spirits, SAP IBP helps keep the global beverage supply flowing smoothly, even when demand spikes or supply disruptions loom. 
  • Over 100 million medical devices delivered annually. Insulin pumps, pacemakers, surgical robotics, diagnostic devices, SAP IBP helps ensure life‑saving technologies reach the patients and providers who depend on them, with precision and reliability 
  • Enough materials planned each year to build 10 million homes. From wood and steel to windows, roofing, and fixtures, SAP IBP supports the reliable supply of essential building materials that shape cities, towns, and communities worldwide. 

These figures aren’t abstract metrics but a reflection of how SAP IBP supports the essentials of modern life. Every drink delivered, every medical device shipped, every home built represents the work planners quietly enable behind the scenes. This is the real value: a planning platform that powers what people rely on every single day. 

 

What's Ahead in 2026: Planning Gets even Smarter 

Looking ahead, SAP IBP is entering a new era of intelligent autonomy. 

  • Agentic AI agents will help planners automate routine tasks, monitor model performance, and surface proactive insights, acting as always-on digital teammates that reduce bottlenecks and speed up decisions. 
  • Harmonized scenario management will bring orders and key figures together into a unified lifecycle, enabling faster scenario creation, comparison, and alignment across teams. 
  • For former SAP APO users, upcoming innovations (including vendor-managed inventory - VMI) and co-/by-product planning on the HPA foundation will make transitions smoother, more intuitive, and richer in capability. 
  • Meanwhile, the HANA Execution Engine (HEX) will further accelerate calculations and AI‑powered workflows, and on-stack extensibility will give you more flexibility to tailor planning without impacting core stability. 

 

To dive deeper into 2025 achievements, customer stories, and the full 2026 roadmap, download the SAP Integrated Business Planning Annual Report 2025 today.

entry.title: Meetup - Pre-SIT Madrid 2026
entry.content:
¡Hola comunidad SAP en Madrid! ¿Qué les parece si nos juntamos la noche antes del SAP Inside Track Madrid 2026? La idea es reunirnos para hacer networking, compartir lo vivido en el CodeJam del mismo día (para los que asistieron) y ponernos en modo "evento" para el SIT del día siguiente. Comida y bebida a cargo de cada uno.

Tenemos una reserva para 15 personas en Restaurante Cazorla, Calle de Juan de Mena, Madrid, Spain. Por favor confirmar la asistencia con la funcionalidad de RSVP de esta pagina.

------

Hola SAP Community in Madrid! Join us the evening before SAP Inside Track Madrid 2026 for an informal dinner and networking session. Great chance to meet fellow SIT attendees, swap stories from the CodeJam earlier that day (for those who attend the CodeJam), and kick off the event week in style. Each person covers their own food and drinks.

entry.title: Mercher &Akqusition -Training bei Big Bang-Integrationen: Erfolgsfaktoren und Praxiserfahrungen
entry.content:
Die Integration von 800 Mitarbeitenden aus 42 Ländern in nur neun Monaten – diese außergewöhnliche Herausforderung stellte sich der Endress+Hauser Gruppe bei der strategischen Partnerschaft mit der SICK AG. Beim Knowledge Café des SAP Learning and Adoption Forums teilten Katja Sommerer, Leading IT Project Manager bei Endress+Hauser, und Can Özhan, Learning Service Senior Specialist bei SAP, ihre Erfahrungen aus diesem komplexen M&A-Projekt und demonstrierten praxiserprobte Lösungsansätze für scheinbar unlösbare Herausforderungen.

Die Ausgangslage: Komplexität unter extremem Zeitdruck

Das Joint Venture zwischen den beiden Familienunternehmen im Bereich Gas Measurement Development nahm im Oktober 2023 seinen Anfang. Die Projektdimensionen wuchsen kontinuierlich von ursprünglich 20 auf 42 Länder auf drei Kontinenten. Besonders komplex gestaltete sich die Übernahme in China, wo eine komplette Firma mit all ihren Strukturen übernommen werden musste. Can Özhan, der bereits seit zweieinhalb Jahren SAP-Transformationsprojekte bei Endress+Hauser begleitete, betonte die technologischen Herausforderungen: Die Migration aus einem S/4HANA-Fremdsystem in die komplexe ECC-Systemwelt von Endress+Hauser unter extremen Zeitvorgaben.

Rechtliche Rahmenbedingungen als größtes Hindernis

Die rechtlichen Beschränkungen erwiesen sich als dominantes Hindernis. Bis zum offiziellen Tag 1 der Übernahme war jeglicher direkte Kontakt zu den zukünftigen Kolleginnen und Kollegen untersagt. Dadurch fehlten dem Projektteam wesentliche Einblicke in die tatsächliche Zusammensetzung der Zielgruppe – etwa Rollen, Aufgabenprofile oder Qualifikationen.

Diese fehlende Transparenz erschwerte insbesondere die Durchführung der Learning Needs Analysis. Normalerweise bildet die LNA die Grundlage für eine zielgerichtete Trainingsarchitektur. In diesem Projekt mussten jedoch wesentliche Annahmen getroffen werden, da keinerlei Austausch mit den betroffenen Mitarbeitenden möglich war. Erst nach der Vertragsunterzeichnung konnten begrenzt Informationen bereitgestellt werden – allerdings weiterhin stark eingeschränkt und nur für einen kleinen Personenkreis.

Zentrale Parameter wie Anzahl, Qualifikationen oder Funktionsbereiche blieben daher lange unklar. „Wir wussten ungefähr, dass da 100 in Deutschland und 80 in Indien kommen würden, aber was für Leute das sind – Servicetechniker, Vertriebler oder eine Mischung –, das war völlig offen“, fasst Katja Sommerer die Ausgangslage zusammen.

Strategische Antwort: Das Key-User-Konzept als Rettungsanker

Unter diesen Bedingungen entwickelte das Projektteam eine Trainingsstrategie, die stark auf ein erweitertes Key‑User‑Modell setzte. Statt das Projektteam in alle Länder zu schicken, bauten die Verantwortlichen auf erfahrene Endress+Hauser‑Mitarbeitende, die als Multiplikatoren fungierten. Ein Key User unterstützte jeweils mehrere End User – nicht nur während der Vorbereitung, sondern durchgängig bis zum Ende der Hypercare‑Phase im April 2025. Die Nominierung und Schulung von rund 200 Key Usern zwischen November und Dezember 2024 bedeutete eine enorme Zusatzbelastung, zeigte aber große Wirkung im Projektalltag.

Learning Journeys: Strukturierte Wissensvermittlung trotz Unwägbarkeiten

Ohne klassische LNA musste die Trainingsarchitektur so robust wie flexibel sein. In intensiven Workshops mit den Fachbereichen entstanden rollenbasierte Learning Journeys, die eine klare Struktur boten – robust genug für Unklarheiten, präzise genug für den Alltag.

Die Learning Journey für Standard Sales illustriert die durchdachte Struktur: Nach der Key-User-Schulung in der Vorbereitungsphase folgte ab dem 1. Januar 2025 eine systematische Einarbeitung. Die ersten ein bis zwei Wochen konzentrierten sich auf allgemeine HR-Trainings und Welcome Days, bevor ab Mitte Januar die spezifischen System- und Prozesstrainings mit dreistündigen Prozessschulungen starteten.

SAP Enable Now als technologisches Rückgrat

Da kein Zugriff auf Produktionsdaten möglich war, setzte das Team konsequent auf SAP Enable Now. Sämtliche Trainingsprozesse wurden als realitätsnahe Simulationen aufgebaut, die Enable Now Library diente als zentrale Distributionsplattform.

Der Content-Erstellungszeitraum war extrem begrenzt: Zwischen Mitte September und Ende Oktober mussten alle Trainingsmaterialien fertiggestellt werden. Um die Prozessexperten zu entlasten, wurden Content Creators über SAP engagiert – eine Entscheidung, die zwar teuer war, aber unter dem Zeitdruck "hervorragend funktioniert" hat, so Katja Sommerer.

Erfolgsfaktoren und organisatorische Unterstützung

Der Projekterfolg basierte auf mehreren kritischen Faktoren. "Wir hatten Top Management Attention auf Tagesbasis. Alle involvierten Mitarbeiter weltweit handelten konzentriert darauf, diese 860 Menschen an Bord zu nehmen", erläuterte Katja Sommerer die organisatorische Unterstützung.

Die Nutzung bestehender Community-Strukturen erwies sich als besonders klug. Fulfillment-, Logistik- und Finance-Communities wurden um neue Mitarbeitende erweitert und durch Projektteam-Mitglieder verstärkt. Der Austausch erfolgte über Microsoft Teams-Channels, wobei regionale Vernetzung besonders erfolgreich war.

Ein durchdachtes Ticketing-System sorgte für klare Eskalationswege: "Wenn ihr das Gefühl habt, das ist ein IT-Thema, dann macht ein Ticket auf. Wenn ihr bloß nicht wisst, wie es geht, dann nutzt die Community." Diese Logik bewährte sich trotz anfänglicher Skepsis überraschend gut.

Herausforderungen bei der Rollenzuordnung

Die Zuordnung neuer Mitarbeitender zu vordefinierten Trainingsrollen gestaltete sich komplex. "Die Jobtitel hießen völlig anders als unsere Rollen", beschrieb Katja Sommerer die Situation. Die Lösung lag in einer pragmatischen Herangehensweise mit flexibler Rollenzuordnung basierend auf Endress+Hauser-Standards, unterstützt durch öffentlich zugängliche Trainingsinhalte.

Messbare Erfolgsindikatoren

Zum Go-Live-Termin am 1. März 2025 waren alle 860 neuen Mitarbeitenden vollständig integriert: "Jeder hatte seinen Laptop, die passenden Berechtigungen, seine Zugriffsdaten, die Stammdaten waren sauber gepflegt, das Business war übernommen, sie konnten arbeiten." Die Datenintegration gelang trotz komplexer Systemlandschaft ohne größere Störungen, die Hypercare-Phase bis April 2025 verlief erfolgreich.

Skalierbarkeit und Übertragbarkeit

Die Methodik demonstriert, wie komplexeste M&A-Integrationen erfolgreich bewältigt werden können. Die Kombination aus Key-User-Konzept, rollenbasierten Learning Journeys und modernen Trainingstechnologien schafft ein reproduzierbares Framework. Besonders bemerkenswert ist die Flexibilität des Ansatzes, der trotz unvollständiger Informationen funktionierte.

Fazit

Die Erfahrungen von Katja Sommerer und Can Özhan demonstrieren, dass erfolgreiche M&A-Trainings weniger eine Frage der verfügbaren Zeit als vielmehr der strategischen Planung und konsequenten Umsetzung sind. Das Key-User-Konzept erweist sich als wertvoller Baustein für nachhaltige Organisationsentwicklung.

Für Unternehmen vor ähnlichen M&A-Herausforderungen bietet dieses Praxisbeispiel eine gute Blaupause. Die Kombination aus vorausschauender Planung, flexiblen Trainingskonzepten und nachhaltiger Betreuung schafft die Grundlage für erfolgreiche Integrationen – auch unter extremem Zeitdruck und regulatorischen Beschränkungen.

Hier geht es zu den Slides zu der Session. Das Forum 2026 steht auch schon in den Startlöchern - hier geht es zur Agenda und zur Registrierung.

entry.title: Context-Driven Dashboard Experiences: Role-Based Layouts in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
What if a single dashboard could completely change depending on who’s viewing it?

While working on a recent project, I explored how scripting can be used to tailor dashboards dynamically based on user roles. Instead of maintaining multiple versions of the same story, I built a single dashboard that adapts in real time – showing only the widgets relevant to each user.

The approach is straightforward: start with a story where all widgets are hidden by default, then use a script to detect the user’s role on load and reveal the appropriate content. This can be as simple as adding a few extra charts, or as powerful as presenting an entirely different dashboard experience.

In this example, I focused on contextualisation by designing views for two distinct audiences – Sales and Marketing – each with their own KPIs.

The API Reference Guide is a great place to start and understand the scripting possibilities: View API Reference

This blog is the first in a series of exploring applied use cases in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

1. Create the Dashboard

I began by building the Sales view, which includes charts, a contextualised dashboard summary, and dynamic name and team text. Once this was in place, I hid all the containers within the page outline except for the dynamic name and team text.

Next, I created the Marketing view, following the same approach – building out the required components and then hiding all the relevant containers. At this stage, when the story is opened, the dashboard appears blank regardless of the user’s role.

Note: I made sure to label the containers (Sales1, Sales2, etc.) so it was easier to find what I wanted to show. 

 

2. Display Content Based on User Role

With the layout complete, I moved on to scripting.

 

Using the on-initialisation script, I first created a variable to capture the user’s team information. I then defined another variable to extract the specific team name. In addition, I created a global variable called “Team” within the page outline, which allows the team name to be displayed dynamically at the top of the dashboard.

 

var teams = Application.getTeamsInfo();
var teamName = teams[0].name;
Team = teams[0].name;

From there, it was simply a matter of using an if statement to control visibility – setting the appropriate elements to visible based on the user’s team. As a result, when a user logs in, they only see the content relevant to them, with the team name updating dynamically. 

if (teamName === "Sales") {
    Sales.setVisible(true);
    Sales1.setVisible(true);
    Sales2.setVisible(true);
    Sales3.setVisible(true);
    Sales4.setVisible(true);
}

if (teamName === "Marketing") {
    Marketing.setVisible(true);
    Marketing1.setVisible(true);
    Marketing2.setVisible(true);
    Marketing3.setVisible(true);
    Marketing4.setVisible(true);
}

 

There are plenty of opportunities for further customisation here. Beyond just showing or hiding charts, you can tailor buttons, apply filters, and even switch branding to create a more personalised experience.

entry.title: F17P cannot consolidate balance reports across multiple company codes (Ford Blue, Pro, and Model-e).
entry.content:
Currently, Ford utilizes three distinct company codes to represent its business segments: Ford Blue, Ford Pro, and Ford Model-e. Many customers conduct business across all three segments simultaneously.

When the Balance Confirmation process is executed using T-code F17P specifically for Poland and CZECH, the system generates individual reports restricted to a single company code. This results in fragmented reporting for a single customer entity. There is a business requirement to provide these customers with a single, unified balance confirmation statement that aggregates their financial position across all relevant Ford company codes, rather than receiving three separate documents.

  2. BTE (Business Transaction Events): Can a specific BTE (such as those in the 1310 or 1320 se

entry.title: Can the ATC baseline be considered in regular batch runs for the S4HANA_READINESS_2023 check?
entry.content:
Hi All!

We are nearing the end of our manual custom code conversion in the first of two development systems (S/4HANA 7.58) we need to tackle and have only a few "left over" ABAP objects in our worklist. However, many more objects still make an appearance in the weekly S4HANA_READINESS_2023 check. 

When we run the check for one object, we have it consider the baseline and no priority 1 or 2 findings are then found (we ignore priority 3 findings as long as testing doesn't show any issues with them).

When switching to "Unresolved and exempted findings" the items exempted via the baseline are displayed and they fit with the results of the batch run - at least for the spot checks I did:

The variant used for program SATC_CI_CFG_SERIES_SCHEDULE in the weekly batch job doesn't seem to have a corresponding checkbox to "consider baseline":

Is there nonetheless a means to filter out findings which are in the baseline? Or is the only - rough and ready - option to change the message priority to "3" if all or at least most of these irrelevant findings are from the same check?

Thanks for any pointers you might have!

Cheers

Bärbel

entry.title: Error KI154 in Manage My Timesheet: How to Resolve Activity Type and Cost Center Issues
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to share an issue we encountered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that prevented users from recording their working time using the Manage My Timesheet app. This article may help others facing similar problems related to error KI154.

 

Issue:

Some users were unable to record their time in the Manage My Timesheet app. After creating a timesheet entry, the following error message was displayed: 

  • Enter activity type only in conjunction with cost center (KI154) (in English)
  • Geben Sie die Leistungsart nur in Verbindung mit einer Kostenstelle an (KI154)  (in German)

 

 

 

Root Cause:

Due to the setup of the interface between SAP S/4HANA and the HR system, each master data change created a new record while removing the cost center and manager from the previous one. When users attempted to record time for an older time period, that record no longer contained a cost center, which triggered error KI154.

 

Solution:

To resolve the issue, perform the following steps: 
1. Open the Manage Workforce app. 
2. Check the employee’s Work Agreement. 
3. Ensure that a Cost Center is maintained for the relevant time period. 
4. If required, temporarily reverse the changes that removed the cost center. 
5. After the timesheet entries have been recorded, the changes can be applied again.

 

Summary:

Error KI154 occurs when a user records time for a period in which no cost center is assigned in the employee master data. Ensuring that the cost center is correctly maintained for the relevant time slot will resolve the issue. 

References:

  • 2647776 - Manage My Timesheet - Error Enter activity type only in conjunction with cost center. - SAP for Me
  • Solved: In Manage My Timesheet unable to Create Task - SAP Community

entry.title: Two SAP Signavio Process Governance Challenges We Solved in a Real Customer Project
entry.content:
Last week, I sat together with an SAP partner to discuss two concrete implementation questions in an ongoing customer project around SAP Signavio Process Governance.

In our session, we discussed two very practical requirements from a live implementation. Because both topics come directly from project reality, I want to share them here, explain the background, and describe how we solved them.

At its core, SAP Signavio Process Governance is a cloud-based workflow solution for governing processes, risks, and controls. It supports features such as workflow triggers, human tasks and forms, access control, email-based interactions, and approval workflows for process models.

 

Use Case 1: Sending feedback/ a message to the model creator without having enough SAP Signavio Process Governance licenses

The first practical challenge in this customer scenario was the following:

The customer wanted to send feedback from the workflow back to the person who created the BPMN model. At first glance, this sounds straightforward. However, the issue was that not every model creator had a dedicated SAP Signavio Process Governance user or license.

This becomes relevant because workflows in SAP Signavio Process Governance can only automatically resolve personal user attributes such as first name, last name, and email address if the workflow starter exists as a user in the Governance organization. If that is not the case, the system cannot reliably identify the person as an individual recipient for notifications or follow-up emails.

In practice, this creates a common challenge: the business wants to send personalized workflow feedback to the model creator, but it does not want/can not assign a Process Governance license to every potential user.

In this project, we discussed two possible solution approaches.

Option 1: Assign SAP Signavio Process Governance users to the relevant starters

The cleanest and most robust solution is to ensure that the people who start the workflow are also maintained as users in SAP Signavio Process Governance.

This means that the workflow can directly use the standard creator-related fields, such as the case creator’s email address, first name, and last name. As a result, feedback emails can be sent automatically to the correct person without any workaround logic.

This is clearly the best-practice approach, because it gives the customer a stable, system-supported setup and also creates the right foundation for future governance scenarios such as escalations, task assignments, reminders, and auditability.

The downside is obvious: this approach requires enough SAP Signavio Process Governance licenses for the users who are expected to start these workflows.

Option 2: Use a workaround by capturing the requester details in the workflow

If you can not want to provide additional Process Governance licenses, there is still an alternative.

Instead of relying on the system’s case creator information, the workflow can capture the necessary requester information manually through the start form or an early user task. For example, the workflow can include mandatory fields such as:

  • Requestor name
  • Requestor email address

These values are then stored as workflow variables and used later in the process for email notifications.

With this approach, the workflow no longer depends on the person being a licensed Governance user. The email can still be sent from the workflow, but based on the manually entered requester data instead of the system-resolved case creator profile.

This workaround is especially useful in scenarios where many users may trigger approvals, but only a smaller group should hold actual Process Governance licenses.

The trade-off is that this approach is more manual and therefore more error-prone. Users may enter the wrong email address, make spelling mistakes, or provide inconsistent information. Still, for many customer setups, this is a very pragmatic and effective solution.

The process before the change

The process after the change we only needed to add one task and change one existing task.

The normal approval process was enriched with two more fields: 

The added task (send Mail to the requestor/process modeler) 

Key takeaway

So, the core of the first use case is this:

If you want to send workflow feedback to the creator of a BPMN model, but you do not have enough SAP Signavio Process Governance users or licenses for every workflow starter, you have two options:

  • Best practice: license the relevant starters as Governance users
  • Workaround: capture the requester’s email and name manually in the workflow and use these values for notifications

Both approaches work. The right choice depends on whether the customer prioritizes a clean, fully system-driven setup or a leaner licensing model with some manual input.

Use Case 2: Sending rejection feedback or details from the process automatically to the requester

The second practical challenge in this scenario was closely related to the first one.

The customer wanted to ensure that the person who submitted the request also receives direct feedback if the process model is rejected. More specifically, if an approver rejects the BPMN model, the requester should automatically be informed about the reason for the rejection.

This is an important requirement in real governance scenarios. A simple rejection status is often not enough. If model creators or requesters do not understand why a model was rejected, the process quickly becomes inefficient. The request is sent back, but the next revision may still not address the real issue. As a result, approval cycles become longer and more frustrating for everyone involved.

The good news is that this requirement can typically be implemented in SAP Signavio Process Governance without custom development.

The functional idea

The basic design is simple:

When an approver rejects the request, they do not only choose “Rejected”, but also enter a reason for the rejection in a dedicated form field, for example:

  • Rejection reason
  • Feedback to requestor
  • Comment on rejection

This information is then stored as part of the workflow data.

After that, the workflow follows the rejection path and triggers an automatic email task. In this email, the process can include:

  • the rejection status
  • the rejection reason entered by the approver
  • optionally, additional guidance on what needs to be changed before resubmission

This way, the requester receives immediate and structured feedback directly from the workflow.

Why this matters

This may sound like a small detail, but from a business perspective it makes a major difference.

Instead of just informing the requester that the submission was rejected, the workflow closes the communication gap and provides actionable feedback. This improves transparency, reduces unnecessary back-and-forth, and helps the requester revise the BPMN model more efficiently.

In other words, the workflow does not just control approval. It also supports continuous improvement.

Dependency on recipient information

The only important prerequisite is that the workflow must know where to send the email.

This connects directly back to the first use case.

There are again two possible setups:

Option 1: The requester exists as a Governance user

If the requester or workflow starter is maintained as a user in SAP Signavio Process Governance, the workflow can use the standard creator-related user information and send the email directly to that person.

This is the cleanest setup because recipient information is resolved automatically by the system. 

(Spoiler Option 1 is the easiest one and the one I would recommend, because you have with option 2 the same issues like in use case no. 1) 

Option 2: The requester data was captured manually in the workflow

If the requester/receiver  is not a licensed Governance user, the workflow can still send the feedback email, provided that the requester’s email address was captured earlier in the process through a form field.

In that case, the rejection email is not sent to the technical case creator profile, but to the manually maintained requester email stored in the workflow.

This means that the second use case can also be solved without assigning additional Governance licenses, as long as the requester information is collected explicitly at the beginning of the workflow.

The result: 

I reuse the attributes I created in Use Case No.1

The workflow in usage.

In the new task I added the attributes from use case no 1. 

I added also attributes in the text field so the mail has the written feedback from the use case no. 1 simply use "#" and add the field of your choice. 

The mail I got. You see If I have a mistake in the mail--> I don't get the mail or maybe some other errors are in the mail

 

Key takeaway

The core of the second use case is this:

If a submitted BPMN model is rejected, the requester should not only see the rejection result, but also receive the actual feedback explaining the decision.

This can be implemented in SAP Signavio Process Governance by:

  1. adding a rejection comment field in the approval step
  2. storing that feedback in the workflow
  3. sending it automatically in a follow-up email task to the requester

So the process becomes much more than a simple approve-or-reject mechanism. It becomes a structured governance workflow that actively supports communication and better rework cycles.

Final thoughts

To be honest, I believe SAP Signavio Process Governance is a underestimated modul in the SAP Signavio portfolio. Many people primarily associate SAP Signavio with modeling, mining, and analysis and SPG with the approval workflow for BPMN models, but it can do way more e.g. to operationalizing governance, approvals, responsibilities, and workflow-based control, Process Governance is where things become actionable.

For me, this was a reminder that SAP Signavio Process Governance is more than just a technical add-on for approvals.

It is the module that turns governance concepts into executable workflows. It connects process modeling (or other topics) with real accountability, real communication, and real operational follow-through.

And that is exactly why it deserves more attention.

If you are currently working on similar requirements around process approvals, requester handling, or workflow notifications in SAP Signavio Process Governance, these two design patterns are worth keeping in your toolbox.

Best Regards

Jonas Germann 

SAP BTM Solution Advisor

entry.title: Designing File Processing in BTP Integration Suite with Amazon S3 and Apache POI
entry.content:
Introduction

Even in the world of AI and LLMs, file-based interfaces are still very common in enterprise landscape. Legacy systems and external applications often exchange operational data in spreadsheet form, even when the downstream integration landscape is API-driven. This creates an interesting architectural challenge for the middleware layer to perform controlled document-level transformations along with integration.

Recently, I came across a use case where an Excel file placed in an Object Store had to be picked up by SAP Cloud Integration, enriched with additional data and written back. At first glance, this looked like a straightforward file transfer requirement. However, the real question was not about moving the file from one folder to another, but about deciding how the data-level processing should be performed and how much should responsibly sit within the integration layer.

I thought this would be a good opportunity to document my learning through a practical end-to-end scenario. In this blog, I will focus on five key aspects:

  1. Provisioning and using Amazon S3 as the object store through SAP BTP.
  2. Configuring and performing S3 operations through AWS CLI.
  3. Connecting SAP Cloud Integration to the S3 bucket using the AmazonWebServices adapter.
  4. Integrating external open-source libraries (example: Apache POI) into Cloud Integration.
  5. Finally, Groovy script to perform document-level processing and writing the file back to S3.

I have tried to keep it simple and the motive behind this blog is to showcase the possibilities and give a starting point for developers and consultants.

1. Provisioning and using Amazon S3 as the object store through SAP BTP:

I chose to explore SAP BTP Object store as a part of this journey. You can very well substitute it with SFTP server for file based integration as well.  You can very well search for Object Store from the Service Marketplace of your BTP subaccount. Refer below diagram:

 Figure 1: Object Store Service

Create a service instance.This service is specific to the infrastructure layer such as Azure Blob Storage, Amazon Web Services, and Google Cloud Platform. In my case as my BTP subaccount is provisioned on AWS, S3 instance will be created.

Once the instance is successfully created, create a service key with preferred name, for example: sandbox-object-store-service-key. This service key will provide all the metadata for the provisioned S3 bucket.

2. Configuring and performing S3 operations through AWS CLI:

I wanted a simple way to validate the S3 bucket outside SAP Cloud Integration before using it into the iFlow. AWS CLI was the choice because it allowed me to upload, download, list, and delete files directly from the terminal. That made it useful not only for initial setup, but also for end-to-end validation of the overall integration flow.

Installing AWS CLI

brew install awscli
aws --version

If AWS CLI is installed correctly, you should see the version details.

Configuring AWS CLI

aws configure

The above command will ask you to enter below details

  1. Access Key ID
  2. Secret Access Key
  3. Region
  4. Output format (default it to: json)

All the above details can be obtained from the service key which you have created in the above section.

You can check of the attributes are added to the configuration using the below command

aws configure list

Connecting to the S3 Bucket

aws s3 ls "s3://<bucket-name-from-service-key>/inbound/"

This is an important validation step. If the command succeeds, it confirms that:

  • The CLI is installed correctly
  • Credentials are valid
  • Configured endpoint is correct
  • Access policy allows listing the bucket

Uploading a File to S3

I created a excel file which contains some predefined data as shown below

Col1Col2Col3
123
456
789

Using the following command, you can upload the file from your local machine to S3 bucket.

aws s3 cp "/Users/<Directory>/Desktop/Book1.xlsx" "s3://<bucket-name>/inbound/Book1.xlsx"

Once done you can check by listing the files in the inbound directory

aws s3 ls "s3://<bucket-name-from-service-key>/inbound/"

With this, you have successfully configured S3 bucket and uploaded files.

3. Connecting SAP Cloud Integration to the S3 with AmazonWebServices adapter:

To connect Cloud Integration with Amazon S3, SAP provides the AmazonWebServices adapter.  As per the Cloud Integration adapter documentation, this adapter supports S3 for both sender and receiver scenarios. For more details you can check the Business Accelerator Hub

To start with I created an integration flow as shown in the below image

 Figure 2: Integration Flow

The above integration flow performs below tasks:

  1. Leverages "AmazonWebServices" receiver adapter to read file from S3 bucket.
  2. Logs the file metadata
  3. Reads the excel workbook and adds data to the existing row (You can enrich data from external API as well and extend this use case).
  4. Uploads the updated file to the S3 bucket using "AmazonWebServices" receiver adapter for write operation.

For reference, I provided the screenshot of the adapter configuration

 Figure 3: AWS Adapter Config

4. Integrating external open-source libraries (example: Apache POI) into Cloud Integration:

Standard Groovy scripting alone was not sufficient because the requirement was to read and update Excel workbook content directly. That introduced the need for an external library capable of working with spreadsheet structures such as workbooks, sheets, rows, and cells. After further exploring, I used Apache POI, which is a widely used open-source Java library for Microsoft Office document processing.

You can add external archives to be referenced in the groovy script. This makes it possible to bring in third-party libraries when the use case goes beyond what the standard runtime APIs provide. You can do it from the References section as shown below

 Figure 4: Archives

Following JARs are imported in the integration flow:

  1. commons-collections4-4.1.jar
  2. poi-3.17.jar
  3. poi-ooxml-3.17.jar
  4. poi-ooxml-schemas-3.17.jar
  5. xmlbeans-2.6.0.jar

Important Note: A practical example of this pattern is Apache POI, but the same concept can also be applied to other well-scoped open-source libraries when there is a clear functional reason to do so and the additional dependency remains manageable, secured and as per licensing agreements.

5. Groovy script to perform document-level processing and writing the file back to S3.

Once the connectivity to S3 was established and the Apache POI libraries were imported in the integration flow, the data processing logic is implemented in a Groovy script.
The Groovy script shown below is a good starting point to demonstrate data handling and enrichment in SAP Cloud Integration using Apache POI. In this example, I update the excel worksheet by adding a new column (SUM) and populating it with the summation of the first three columns for each data row. Eventually the data is saved in a new file named : Book2.xlsx

import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.Message
import org.apache.poi.ss.usermodel.Cell
import org.apache.poi.ss.usermodel.DataFormatter
import org.apache.poi.ss.usermodel.Row
import org.apache.poi.ss.usermodel.Sheet
import org.apache.poi.ss.usermodel.Workbook
import org.apache.poi.xssf.usermodel.XSSFWorkbook
import java.io.ByteArrayInputStream
import java.io.ByteArrayOutputStream

def Message processData(Message message) {

    byte[] body = message.getBody(byte[].class)
    if (!body) {
        throw new IllegalStateException('Payload is empty')
    }

    Workbook workbook
    try {
        workbook = new XSSFWorkbook(new ByteArrayInputStream(body))
        Sheet sheet = workbook.getSheetAt(0)

        DataFormatter formatter = new DataFormatter()

        int headerRowIndex = sheet.getFirstRowNum()
        Row headerRow = sheet.getRow(headerRowIndex)
        if (headerRow == null) {
            throw new IllegalStateException('Sheet has no header row')
        }

        Map<String, Integer> headerToIndex = buildHeaderIndex(headerRow, formatter)
        Integer col1Idx = headerToIndex.get('col1')
        Integer col2Idx = headerToIndex.get('col2')
        Integer col3Idx = headerToIndex.get('col3')

        if (col1Idx == null || col2Idx == null || col3Idx == null) {
            throw new IllegalStateException("Expected header columns: Col1, Col2, Col3")
        }

        int sumColIdx = col3Idx + 1;
        setCellString(headerRow, sumColIdx, 'SUM')

        int lastRow = sheet.getLastRowNum()
        for (int r = headerRowIndex + 1; r <= lastRow; r++) {
            Row row = sheet.getRow(r)
            if (row == null) continue

            double v1 = readNumeric(row.getCell(col1Idx, Row.MissingCellPolicy.RETURN_BLANK_AS_NULL))
            double v2 = readNumeric(row.getCell(col2Idx, Row.MissingCellPolicy.RETURN_BLANK_AS_NULL))
            double v3 = readNumeric(row.getCell(col3Idx, Row.MissingCellPolicy.RETURN_BLANK_AS_NULL))
            double sum = v1 + v2 + v3

            Cell sumCell = row.getCell(sumColIdx, Row.MissingCellPolicy.CREATE_NULL_AS_BLANK)
            sumCell.setCellValue(sum)
        }

        ByteArrayOutputStream out = new ByteArrayOutputStream(Math.max(body.length, 1024))
        workbook.write(out)
        message.setBody(out.toByteArray())
        message.setHeader("CamelFileName", "Book2.xlsx")
        message.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet')
        return message
    } finally {
        if (workbook != null) {
            workbook.close();
        }
    }
}

def Map<String, Integer> buildHeaderIndex(Row headerRow, DataFormatter formatter) {
    Map<String, Integer> result = new HashMap<>()
    short last = headerRow.getLastCellNum()
    if (last < 0) return result

    for (int c = 0; c < last; c++) {
        Cell cell = headerRow.getCell(c, Row.MissingCellPolicy.RETURN_BLANK_AS_NULL)
        if (cell == null) continue
        String name = formatter.formatCellValue(cell)
        if (name == null) continue
        name = name.trim().toLowerCase()
        if (!name) continue
        result.put(name, c)
    }
    return result
}

def void setCellString(Row row, int colIdx, String value) {
    Cell cell = row.getCell(colIdx, Row.MissingCellPolicy.CREATE_NULL_AS_BLANK)
    cell.setCellValue(value)
}

def double readNumeric(Cell cell) {
    if (cell == null) return 0d
    return cell.getNumericCellValue()
}

Once the additional data is added to the worksheet, last step is to write back the file in the S3 bucket using AWS receiver adapter with write operations. The connection properties remain same as before. The processing properties are updated as shown in the below image

 Figure 5: AWS S3 Write File

Once you deploy the integration flow and completes successfully, you should see a new file in your S3 bucket.

Download the file from S3 on your local using below command

aws s3 cp "s3://<S3-bucket-name>/inbound/Book2.xlsx" "/Users/<Directory>/Desktop/Book2.xlsx"

You should see a fourth column "SUM" as shown below

Col1Col2Col3SUM
1236
45615
78924

Important Considerations

This approach works well in a controlled environment with smaller dataset, there are few important points worth keeping in mind

  1. Use compatible JAR versions: Open source libraries should be compatible with the JAVA runtime.
  2. Keep an eye on security vulnerabilities: Open source libraries might come with some security vulnerabilities.
  3. Plan for upgrades carefully: Test your integrations thoroughly if you upgrade the JAR files or if there is platform upgrades. 
  4. Be cautious with large files: Since the workbook is processed in memory, there is a possibility of higher memory consumption and potential out-of-memory situations for large datasets. In such cases, it is better to split the file in smaller sizes and then process it.
  5. Exception Handling: Ensure robust exception handling and alerting during development.

Wrap-Up

At this point, we have looked at:

  1. Provisioning and using Amazon S3 for the integration scenario
  2. Performing basic S3 operations through AWS CLI
  3. Connecting SAP Cloud Integration to Amazon S3 using the AmazonWebServices adapter
  4. Integrating external open-source libraries such as Apache POI into Cloud Integration
  5. Using Groovy and Apache POI to enrich an Excel workbook and write the updated file back to S3

This exercise was a practical way to explore how SAP Cloud Integration can handle document-level processing in addition to file movement. While the example itself was intentionally simple, the pattern opens up interesting possibilities for data enrichment scenarios within the integration layer.

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/amazonwebservices-receiver-adapter 
  2. https://api.sap.com/package/AmazonWebServicesAdapter/overview 
  3. https://poi.apache.org/ 
  4. https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/using-script-api-methods-in-groovy-scripts

entry.title: TPM10 is generating '9000 flows' for deposits
entry.content:
We are unable to resolve an error that occurs when executing TPM10 for deposit transaction.

The system indicates that the flow does not have a payment currency maintained, but we are unable to determine which currency is missing.

When executing transaction TPM10 with the following fields:

These are capitalized interest flows:

Question 1: Why does a deposit need a 9000 Derived business transaction flow?
The error which appears in Account Determination with the message: “Flow has no payment currency.”

Question 2: Why this payment currency error is happening just in this deposit?
The movement generating this issue is DBT_C004 – Negative Amortization (for Inflow/Outflow).
We are also unsure whether this flow should be generated at all for a deposit transaction.

DBT_C004 this is happening in PRO but in CUSTOMIZING we find the same error in DBT_C002.

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: Data engineer - SAP HANA
entry.content:
Getting certified as a data engineer on SAP HANA Cloud can feel like a big step.  The content is deep, the exam is detailed, and time is always tight.

That’s exactly why we created this Partner Learning Accelerator.

In this 3-hour live session, you’ll walk through key topics like data modeling, provisioning, and exam structure. You’ll see real examples, ask questions in real time, and get practical tips that go beyond slides. Plus, we’ll close with a focused exam wrap-up so you know what to expect.

If you’re working with SAP HANA Cloud or planning to, this is a strong way to build confidence before the C_HAMOD certification.

The thing is, preparation becomes much easier when you know where to focus.

Join us and move one step closer to certification.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013386.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPHANA #DataEngineering #SAPLearning #CloudSkills

entry.title: Re: I have no subscription option for Workzone
entry.content:
Hi @TimurKalynovskyi 

 

You have to add the entitlement in your global account. Add the Plan as Standard (Application) for BWZ. Please take reference as below:

 

Work Zone subscription is not related to CIS. You can Integrate any tenant of the CIS in your Sub Account. But to Subscribe to Work Zone application you should be having a tenant in the Trust configuration.

Try to add the entitlement in Global Account and then add the WZ subscription (It will come)

 

If you want to change the tenant then first remove the existing tenant and then add the another tenant in the Trust and after that Subscribe to WZ.

 

I hope this will fix your issue.

 

Please mark this as answer if your issue will get fixed or let me know if you have any issue.

 

Regards,

Rohit Gera

entry.title: Re: Application Job parameters do not refresh
entry.content:
For everyone still struggling with the missing refresh of the application job in 2023 like myself: 

* create another implementation class inheriting from your implementation

* whenever you change the "metadata" (e.g. parameters), switch between the two class names (the actual implementation and the class to inherit) in the application job catalog entry and activate.

* when you are done with development simply delete the inheriting class and stay with the original one

This reduces the efforts significantly.
