entry.title: Joule A2A: Secure Code Based Agents with IAS
entry.content:
In the previous blog post, we explored how to integrate a Pro-Code Agent into the Joule ecosystem. While the initial setup functioned correctly, it did not include proper authentication, leaving the agent accessible to anyone on the internet.

In this post, we address that gap by implementing secure access controls. Specifically, we will demonstrate how to protect your agent using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service (IAS) in a Python-based setup.

Scenario

Our target is to use SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication Service (short: IAS) to take care of the authentication. We will implement the authentication check on the Python side using middleware that validates JWT tokens from IAS.

Important distinction: In this blog, we focus on protecting the agent from unauthorized access using the OAuth2 Client Credentials flow. This is app-to-app authentication - we verify that the calling application (e.g., Joule) has valid credentials to access our agent. The token identifies the application, not the end user.

If your agent needs to call backend systems on behalf of the actual user (e.g., accessing SAP S/4HANA with the user's permissions), you'll need Principal Propagation - forwarding the user's identity through the call chain. That's a different pattern which I'll cover in a follow-up blog post.

By the end of this blog, you will have a secure A2A agent that validates JWT tokens and IAS configured with the necessary applications and API scopes.


Prerequisites

For the IAS setup basics, please refer to my previous blog post: Using IAS to Secure Python APIs on Cloud Foundry. That post covers:

  • Creating an application in IAS
  • Configuring Provided APIs (scopes like api_read_access)
  • Creating client credentials with API permissions

So please follow the chapter "Creating an Application in IAS" closely and then get started here.

For this scenario, you will need:

  1. IAS Tenant: Access to an SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant with an application configured
  2. Client Credentials: Client ID and Client Secret with api_read_access scope
  3. BTP Cloud Foundry: A Cloud Foundry space to deploy the agent
  4. SAP AI Core: For the agent's LLM capabilities (optional)

The overall setup of the A2A Integration is explained in my previous blog post regarding A2A. We are building upon that one here.


Part 1: Understanding JWT Token Verification

Before diving into the code, let's understand what we're actually verifying. When a client authenticates with IAS using the client credentials flow, IAS returns a JWT (JSON Web Token). This token is a Base64-encoded JSON object with three parts: header, payload, and signature.

Here's what a decoded IAS token payload looks like:

{
  "ias_apis": [
    "api_read_access",
    "api_write_access"
  ],
  "sub": "<your-client-id>",
  "aud": "<your-client-id>",
  "sap_id_type": "app",
  "azp": "<your-client-id>",
  "iss": "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com",
  "exp": 1775566673,
  "iat": 1775563073,
  "jti": "0324db00-39ab-4dc5-888c-11fd4959aef5"
}

The key fields our middleware needs to verify are: iss (issuer) confirms the token came from our IAS tenant, aud (audience) confirms the token is meant for our application, exp (expiration) ensures the token hasn't expired, and ias_apis contains the scopes we configured in IAS - this is where we check for api_read_access.

The token is cryptographically signed by IAS. To verify this signature, our middleware fetches the public keys from IAS's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint at /oauth2/certs. This ensures nobody can forge a valid token - only IAS can create tokens that pass verification.


Part 2: The IAS Authentication Middleware

Now let's look at the implementation. The middleware is intentionally minimal - about 50 lines of code:

"""IAS Authentication Middleware for A2A Server."""

import os

import jwt
import requests
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

# Config - Load from environment variables
ISSUER = os.environ.get("IAS_ISSUER", "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com")
JWKS_URL = f"{ISSUER}/oauth2/certs"
AUDIENCE = os.environ.get("IAS_AUDIENCE", "your-client-id-here")


class IASAuthMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    """Validates JWT tokens from SAP IAS."""

    def get_public_key(self, token: str):
        """Fetch JWKS and find the matching public key."""
        kid = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)["kid"]
        jwks = requests.get(JWKS_URL).json()

        for key in jwks["keys"]:
            if key["kid"] == kid:
                return jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(key)

        raise Exception("No matching key found")

    def verify_token(self, token: str):
        """Validate JWT token and return payload."""
        public_key = self.get_public_key(token)
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token, public_key, algorithms=["RS256"],
            audience=AUDIENCE, issuer=ISSUER
        )

        if "api_read_access" not in payload.get("ias_apis", []):
            raise Exception("Missing required ias_apis scope")

        return payload

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        # Allow public discovery endpoints
        if request.url.path.startswith("/.well-known/"):
            return await call_next(request)

        auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")

        if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
            return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": "Missing token"})

        token = auth_header.split(" ")[1]

        try:
            payload = self.verify_token(token)
            request.state.user = payload
        except Exception as e:
            return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": str(e)})

        return await call_next(request)

The get_public_key method fetches the JWKS from IAS and finds the key matching the token's kid (key ID) header. The verify_token method uses PyJWT to decode and verify the token in one step - it checks the signature, issuer, audience, and expiration automatically. We then add our custom check for the api_read_access scope in the ias_apis claim. The dispatch method is called for every request and either lets it through (for /.well-known/ paths) or validates the Bearer token.

Note: Some endpoint are meant to be public - /.well-known/ contains the agent cards and is used for the discovery. Thus we want to exclude that from the auth checks.


Part 3: Integrating with the A2A Server

Adding the middleware to your A2A server is straightforward - just one line:

from a2a.server.apps import A2AStarletteApplication
from middleware.ias_auth import IASAuthMiddleware

# ... setup agent_card and request_handler ...

server = A2AStarletteApplication(
    agent_card=agent_card, http_handler=request_handler
)

# Build app and add IAS auth middleware
app = server.build()
app.add_middleware(IASAuthMiddleware)


Deployment Configuration

Set the environment variables in your manifest.yaml:

applications:
- name: currency-agent
  memory: 512M
  buildpack: python_buildpack
  command: uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT}
  env:
    IAS_ISSUER: "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com"
    IAS_AUDIENCE: "your-client-id"

The IAS_ISSUER is relating to your IAS tenant URL. The IAS_AUDIENCE to the respective client. In our setup this matches to the client_id of the application we maintained in IAS.

Deploy with:

cd app
cf push


Part 4: Testing the Authentication

Let's verify that our authentication is working correctly.


Step 1: Test Public Endpoint (No Auth Required)

The agent card is accessible without authentication for A2A discovery:

curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/.well-known/agent.json" | jq '.'

Response:

{
  "name": "Currency Agent",
  "description": "Helps with exchange rates for currencies",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "protocolVersion": "0.3.0",
  "capabilities": {
    "pushNotifications": true,
    "streaming": true
  },
  "skills": [
    {
      "id": "convert_currency",
      "name": "Currency Exchange Rates Tool",
      "description": "Helps with exchange values between various currencies"
    }
  ]
}


Step 2: Test Without Token (Should Fail)

We use the JSON-RPC syntax for doing a HTTP request - sending a basic message construct.

curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "message/send", "params": {}, "id": "1"}'

Response:

As expected - we get the HTTP 401 - Missing Token Error we implemented.

{
  "detail": "Missing token"
}


Step 3: Get Token and Call Agent

Using our IAS Tenant URL to retrieve a Token giving our client credentials. Then doing an actual message send via the A2A syntax. This time we actually include some text message.

# Get token from IAS
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST "https://<tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=<client-id>" \
  -d "client_secret=<client-secret>" | jq -r '.access_token')

# Call the agent
curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/send",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "What is 100 USD in EUR?"}],
        "messageId": "msg-123"
      }
    },
    "id": "1"
  }' | jq '.'

Response:

{
  "id": "1",
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "result": {
    "artifacts": [
      {
        "artifactId": "ad8b315f-dd87-45ca-9f5e-584439102732",
        "name": "conversion_result",
        "parts": [
          {
            "kind": "text",
            "text": "100 USD is approximately 86.77 EUR based on the current exchange rate."
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "contextId": "095d62bd-dfa1-4b77-991b-392fe7038ecd",
    "id": "639c1a4b-d486-45df-b4be-4b614e0c441d",
    "kind": "task",
    "status": {
      "state": "completed",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-07T11:44:14.740338+00:00"
    }
  }
}

And as a result we actually get the converted currency - presenting the auth header!

Part 5: Connecting to Joule

Now that your agent is secured with IAS authentication, you need to configure Joule to communicate with it via a BTP Destination. Last time we configured the Destination without any authentication. This time we opt for OAuth2ClientCredentials.

Create the BTP Destination

In your BTP Cockpit, create a destination for your secured agent:

1. Navigate to Connectivity > Destinations
2. Create a new destination with these settings:
- Name: CURRENCY_AGENT
- Type: HTTP
- URL: https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com
- Proxy Type: Internet
- Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
- Client ID: <Your IAS Client ID>
- Client Secret: <Your IAS Client Secret>
- Token Service URL: https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

Note: Since this blog builds upon the last one - ensure that you have already created a cabability in Joule that points a agent-request towards this destination. Once you created / updated this destination - make sure to update the Joule Capability. Because Joule is apparently caching the Destination content for some time. You can use joule delete and joule deploy to do so!

Test from Joule

Once the destination is configured, you can interact with the secured agent through Joule:

 


Conclusion

With just ~50 lines of middleware code, we've secured our A2A agent with IAS authentication. The middleware validates JWT tokens from IAS, checks the required scopes, and keeps the agent discovery endpoints public for A2A protocol compliance. The full code is available on GitHub.

I hope you found this insightful. Feel free to leave questions in the comments!

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: AI into UI5, SAP Sapphire tips and New Enhancements based on your feedback!
entry.content:
April has just started, and with the new season comes some very exciting news for our SAP Community!

From practical ways to bring AI directly into SAPUI5 and automate deployments in SAP Integration Suite, to ETL best practices in SAP Datasphere, there’s a lot to explore in today’s edition. And, beyond the technical side, we also have announcements about new SAP Community website enhancements. Spoiler: most of these updates were shaped by your feedback, so we’re sure you will appreciate them!

Need tips on building your SAP Sapphire agenda? Or looking for recommendations on local SAP community meetups? We’ve got you covered with a full list of upcoming April events happening around the world.

Let’s dive in!

Featured Blog Posts 

AI into SAPUI5 as reusable controls 

SAP Mentor Wouter Lemaire, published a very interesting approach in his latest blog post, exploring how AI can be embedded directly into SAPUI5 controls through a reusable library. Instead of focusing on complex AI agents, he brings AI closer to where users interact daily, enabling simple tasks like text improvement, translation, or spell checking directly inside UI5 apps using standard data binding-without requiring developers to manage AI infrastructure. The post walks through the available controls, architecture, and design decisions, showing how this lightweight and practical approach can make AI integration more accessible and effective for everyday SAP use cases. 

Read Wouter Blog post here 

UI5 Plugins for Claude 

SAP Product and Topic Expert Florian Vogt introduced the new UI5 plugins for Claude and how to bring AI-powered development directly into SAPUI5 and OpenUI5 workflows. Designed to enhance developer productivity, these open-source plugins enable Claude Code to understand UI5-specific patterns, APIs, and best practices providing more accurate and context-aware assistance without relying on generic AI knowledge. The initial release includes a plugin for general UI5 development support, and another focused on TypeScript migration, helping developers create projects, fix errors, and modernize applications with confidence. If you're working with UI5 and exploring AI-assisted development, this is definitely one to check out. 

Read Florian Blog post here  

SAP Integration Suite automatic deployments using Ansible 

In this practical blog post, SAP Community member Marian Vatafu shared how to automate deployments in SAP Integration Suite using Ansible, reducing manual effort and minimizing human errors across environments. By defining deployment steps once in a YAML instruction file, developers can automatically handle tasks like credential creation, certificate management, artifact deployment, and configuration updates across DEV to PROD. The solution integrates tools like ServiceNow, GitHub, and Jenkins to ensure full traceability, auditability, and secure handling of sensitive data through Ansible Vault. With this approach, teams can streamline operations, improve consistency, and confidently manage complex integration landscapes with significantly less manual work.  

Read Marian Blog post here  

SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL)   

SAP Product and Topic Expert Jeet Kapase shared in his last blog post a guide on best practices for implementing ETL (Extract, Transform & Load) processes in SAP Datasphere, helping data engineers choose the right approach based on performance, scalability, and business needs. The post breaks down the available features-such as Replication Flow, Transformation Flow, Data Flow, and Open SQL Procedures, and explains when to use each one, highlighting SAP's recommendation to prioritize Replication and Transformation Flows for most scenarios. With clear comparisons, real performance insights, and practical guidance, this is a valuable read for anyone looking to design efficient, future-ready data pipelines in SAP Datasphere. 

Read Jeet Blog post here 

Community News 

SAP Community Enhancements  

April started with great news for our SAP Community users, as we have just launched new enhancements to make the experience navigating even better. 

  • Dark Mode:   Dark Mode is now live as the Beta Phase is complete! Last year, after listening to your feedback, we launched Dark Mode in beta, giving you the chance to choose your preferred visual experience. Since then, you have shared suggestions on how to make this feature more polished and seamless. With that in mind, we are happy to introduce the following improvements:

1. Light/Dark Mode Toggle -  A new toggle replaces the temporary text-based switch, making it easier to switch between themes for a more personalized browsing experience. 

2. Dark Mode Improvements for DSA Reporting Form -  The "Report Inappropriate Content" form has been updated to ensure all table content is visible and readable in dark mode. Previously unreadable white-on-white elements now follow the correct dark theme styling. 

 

  • Improved Recent Activity Filters: Now the Recent Activity feed filters on the Community landing page (Most Viewed, Most Commented, Most Liked) show results from the last 30 days, making content discovery more relevant. When you filter for most commented, for example, it only shows the most commented posts from the last 30 days. This makes it nicer and easier to find relevant content. 

 

  • Removal of ‘Users Online’ Homepage Stats: The “Online Members” statistic has been removed from the homepage to simplify the layout and reduce noise, helping users focus on key community conte

Before:

After:

Events 

SAP Sapphire 2026 

Are you planning to attend the SAP Sapphire 2026? This huge event is where SAP makes big announcements about its vision for the future of ERP and business applications. This includes showing new products, presenting customers who have transformed their business using SAP’s solutions, and highlighting new technologies that will shape the future. With so many great speakers and topics, choosing the sessions to attend sometimes can be overwhelming. To help you, SAP Product and Topic Expert Peter Spielvogel, published a blog post with his AI and UX sessions agenda selections. We highly recommend checking out, because he shared a very diverse agenda with an amazing selection of speakers! 

Read Peter Blog post here  

April upcoming Events: 

Talking about planning, have you selected any community driven events to attend this month? Any meetups with your local SAP community group?  In case not, we have got you covered! 

Check the list of the events that are already available on our Events page for you to sign up. There are opportunities all over the world: 

 SAP Stammtisch Wien - 09.04.2026 - SAP Community 

SAP Inside Track Campinas - 11.04.2026 - SAP Community 

SAP Meetup at UATE- 15.04.2026 - SAP Community 

Katowice - SAP Stammtisch 15.04.2026 - SAP Community 

SAP Community Meetup Lisbon - 16.04.2026- SAP Community 

SAP Inside Track Madrid 2026 - 17.04. 2026 - SAP Community 

SAP Stammtisch Wroclaw - 17.04.2026 - SAP Community 

Networking Meetup Dublin - 21.04.2026 - SAP Community 

Basel-Freiburg Stammtisch - 23.04.2026 - SAP Community 

SAP Women Walk & Talk São Paulo- 25.04.2026 - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Change User ID in SAP Cloud Identity Services
entry.content:
Hi Tobi,

unfortunately, you cannot change the user ID (starting with "P") in your SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant. However, you can change the Global User ID. But best practice is to use the Global User ID generated by IAS.

One option is to simplify your architecture and use just a single SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant. This should be sufficient (even for a productive environment), as long as you don’t frequently reconfigure your corporate IdP.

There are other approaches as well - depending on your landscape, but from my perspective, it's important to address this early and establish a stable architecture. Otherwise, you may end up dealing with multiple shadow identity management systems that require ongoing maintenance.

Best regards
René

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4 HANA Cloud, SAP MDG, Business PartnerDelete M...
entry.content:
Dear @Simon,

The released inbound service in this area is Key Mapping Replicate from Client to SAP S/4HANA Cloud. SAP documents the generic ActionCode concept for key mapping structures but for this inbound SOAP service SAP states that it supports only the value used to create data if it does not exist and change it if it exists. In other words this interface should not be positioned as a supported delete service. SAP also states that the publicly available APIs for Public Cloud are published on SAP Business Accelerator Hub based on the currently published interface set there is no SAP-documented released bulk-delete API for this DRF BP key mapping requirement

So there is no supported functional bulk-delete workaround in standard Public Cloud. The practical approach is to first correct or stop the replication issue that created the unwanted mappings, then use Manage Key Mapping for low-volume manual cleanup. For high-volume cleanup the defensible recommendation is to raise an SAP incident and ask SAP for a supported tenant-level cleanup approach

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/e3ccefc938a5408b8a92732c5e88462b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/066da317588f46489f2d8ac8cd572b6c/3e3474defe12486a990eeeec8e2dab3f.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/1e60f14bdc224c2c975c8fa8bcfd7f3f.html

Summary:

For Public Cloud this should be treated as a current standard limitation. The supported path is manual cleanup for small volumes for large-scale cleanup the recommended route is an SAP incident, not an unsupported technical deletion approach

Additional information:

SAP does document Delete Vendor Key Mapping but only in the PLM System Integration context, so it should not be treated as the standard solution for generic DRF Business Partner key mapping cleanup

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f369b2eff700401494ba6e7c9a573288/a2a2fdd4cef14a0f8c16d8262435dc6a.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Fetch Production Order after reading QM Notification...
entry.content: In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, you cannot use classic BADIs like in on‑premise; instead, you must rely on released CDS views, APIs, and key user extensibility to achieve this. The correct approach is to read the serial number from the notification reference, fetch the production order via the released CDS view I_SerialNumberManufacturingOrd, and then update the QM notification using the In‑App Extensibility framework (custom logic or RAP extension)

entry.title: Re: Career with SAP S4/Hana- I have 7 years of experience...
entry.content:
With 7 years in SAP Ariba front‑end, the most strategic next step is to build expertise in SAP S/4HANA Sourcing & Procurement. This path complements your Ariba background, is in high demand globally, and offers a structured certification roadmap (C_TS452 exam). Once you establish S/4HANA procurement skills, you can position yourself as a hybrid consultant bridging Ariba and S/4HANA, which is rare and highly valued.

So the recommended roadmap for you is

Step 1 – Strengthen Ariba Foundation (short term)
Ensure mastery of Ariba Sourcing, Procurement, Contracts, and Ariba Network. Get certified in SAP Ariba Sourcing & Procurement if not already. This consolidates your 7 years of experience into a formal credential.

Step 2 – Transition to S/4HANA Procurement (medium term)
Target SAP Certified Associate – S/4HANA Sourcing & Procurement (C_TS452). Learn procurement fundamentals in S/4HANA: purchase requisitions, purchase orders, goods receipt, invoice mgmt. Training resources for this are: SAP Learning Hub, SAP Knowledge Factory, ERP Q&A guides.

Step 3 – Build Hybrid Expertise (long term)
Learn integration between Ariba and S/4HANA (Ariba Cloud Integration Gateway). Position yourself as a Procurement Transformation Consultant who can deliver end‑to‑end solutions. This is where demand is strongest. Companies want consultants who understand both worlds

entry.title: Re: Fetch Production Order after reading QM Notification...
entry.content:
Hi inamullah,

Are you trying to act on a created Quality Notification?

Could you provide a few more details about your use case?

You have a couple of options here are various levels.

Here are the list of Badis: Help Link 

Potentially this one: Process Data After Saving Notification (BADI_QQM_NOTIF_EVENT_POST_CLD)   or

Check/Change Notification When Saving (BADI_QQM_NOTIF_EVENT_SAVE_CLD)

If that doesn't do what you need, you can look at Developer Extensibility; there are CDS Views and Business Objects giving you access to the Quality Notification: 

https://api.sap.com/bointerface/I_QLTYNOTIFICATIONTP

https://api.sap.com/cdsviews/I_QLTYNOTIFICATION

Finally, you can implement a side by side extensibility:

1. Get the event mesh notification: https://api.sap.com/event/CE_QUALITYNOTIFICATIONEVENTS/resource

2. Use the public apis: https://api.sap.com/api/CE_QUALITYNOTIFICATION_0001/overview

I hope this helps

Thank you

Jerry

entry.content: Quick tip for anyone running into issues installing the plugins: the installer currently clones via SSH, so if you don't have an SSH key configured for github.com, you'll get a Permission Denied error. To fix this, add a public SSH key to your GitHub account under Settings > SSH and GPG keys. Once configured, the installation should work without issues.

entry.title: Re: Mobile App - 1H 2026 Product Release Question
entry.content:
Thanks for the question, Chris. Quick Actions and Quick Links cannot be controlled by MDM, i.e. your MDM cannot pass parameters to the mobile app that specify which Quick Actions and Quick Links will be displayed. This is controlled by the Admin (using Manage Home Page in the Admin Center) and the end user (using Show/Hide capabilities in the app itself).

I hope this answers your question. If not, add a comment and let's discuss!

entry.title: Automatic Payment Allocation (Vendor) not working after upload of Bank Statement (XML, CAMT V8)
entry.content:
Hello together,

using SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, we would expect the automatic payment allocation after uploading a bank statement in CAMT-Format to work from the beginning right away. We have matching Vendor/Supplier and matching Bank Account Data both in the Business Partner Master Data and the Bank Statement File.

This is the starting situation:

In the supplier master data, the exact name and bank account of the supplier are configured ("IBAN", "SWIFT"). In the uploaded Bank file the exact name and IBAN of the supplier are mentioned in the correct fields of the file (XML, CAMT V8).

After the upload (with "Manage Bank Statements" App) the first check of the bank statment line items shows:

S/4HANA Cloud recognizes the IBAN and the Swift Code of the business partner (supplier) correctly. But it does not assign the correct Business Partner / Supplier in the Bank Statement Line Item. No open item for the payment is created or assigned/cancelled out to any open Item in the Vendor Account.

Partner Name, Payment Reference are both empty, Payment Amount is 0.00, Posting Status is "not completed" in the Bank Statement Line Item created in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Does the automatic payment allocation need more information? How can the automatic payment allocation be started correctly? Did we miss Configuration Steps in our System in order to get payment allocation running?

Thank You very much for Your help

entry.title: OAuth 2.0 Authentication in SAP Cloud Integration – A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

Authentication Overview

Authentication is a fundamental concept in system integration, ensuring that only authorized clients can access protected resources.

One commonly used method is Basic Authentication, where credentials are sent with each request. In this approach, the username and password are combined in the following format:

username:password

 This value is then encoded using Base64 and included in the HTTP header:

 Authorization: Basic <encoded_value>

Note: Some fields in the screenshots have been intentionally hidden using white masking boxes to protect sensitive information.

The screenshot above illustrates how Basic Authentication is configured in Postman by providing the client ID as the username and the client secret as the password. Once the request is sent, Postman automatically generates the Authorization header. 

However, this method has an important security limitation. Base64 is only an encoding mechanism, not an encryption method. If the request is intercepted, the Authorization header can be decoded, and the original credentials can be exposed.

In our case, decoding the Basic Authorization header reveals the client credentials directly. This demonstrates why Basic Authentication is not suitable for secure integrations and why a more secure mechanism such as OAuth 2.0 is required.

As shown above, decoding the Basic Authorization header reveals the original client credentials. This demonstrates that Basic Authentication does not provide sufficient protection for secure integrations.

For this reason, a more secure authentication mechanism such as OAuth 2.0 is required.

OAuth 2.0 in SAP Cloud Integration

OAuth 2.0 is a secure, token-based authentication mechanism used to protect APIs and integration endpoints. In SAP Cloud Integration, it is commonly used to secure inbound and outbound communication without exposing client credentials in every request.

Instead of sending the client ID and client secret each time a resource is requested, the client first authenticates against an authorization server and obtains an access token. This token is then used to access the protected Cloud Integration endpoint.

This approach provides several advantages:

  • Sensitive credentials are not repeatedly exposed
  • Access tokens have a limited lifetime
  • Access control is handled through a more secure authentication model

The main elements involved in this process are:

  • Client ID: identifies the calling application
  • Client Secret: authenticates the client
  • Access Token: temporary credential used to access the resource

This token-based approach makes OAuth 2.0 significantly more secure than Basic Authentication.

OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Flow

The OAuth 2.0 flow used in this scenario is the Client Credentials flow, which is well suited for system-to-system communication.

In this model, the client application authenticates itself directly with the authorization server using its client ID and client secret. Once the credentials are validated, the authorization server issues an access token.

The client then uses this token to call the protected Cloud Integration endpoint.

The flow can be summarized as follows:

  1. The client sends its credentials to the authorization server
  2. The authorization server validates the credentials
  3. An access token is generated and returned
  4. The client sends a new request to the Cloud Integration endpoint using the token
  5. The resource server validates the token and returns the requested resource

This flow ensures that the original client credentials are only used to request the token and are not sent to the protected business endpoint itself.

SAP BTP Endpoints Configuration

Several endpoints are involved in the OAuth 2.0 authentication process in SAP BTP and SAP Cloud Integration.

The first one is the Authorization Server URL (tokenurl), which is responsible for generating the access token.

This is the endpoint that receives the client credentials and returns the token if authentication is successful.

https://<subaccount>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

The token request uses the following grant type:

  • grant_type=client_credentials

The protected Cloud Integration resources are then exposed through dedicated runtime endpoints, such as:

  • HTTP endpoint

https://<tenant>/http/...

  • SOAP endpoint

https://<tenant>/cxf/...

The authorization server issues the token, while the Cloud Integration endpoint acts as the resource server and validates the token before returning the requested result.

In our case, the authentication flow works as follows: the application validates the client ID and client secret by calling the authorization server.

If the credentials are correct, the authorization server returns an access token with an expiration time.

This token is then used to call the Cloud Integration resource endpoint. If the token is valid, the requested resource is returned.

Token Generation and Usage

Once the authorization server validates the client ID and client secret, it returns an OAuth 2.0 access token. This token becomes the credential used to access the protected Cloud Integration endpoint.

A typical token response contains values such as:

  • access_token
  • token_type
  • expires_in

After the token is generated, it must be included in the HTTP request header using the Bearer scheme: Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

This replaces the previous Basic Authentication header:

 Authorization: Basic <encoded_value>

The key difference is that the client secret is no longer sent when accessing the Cloud Integration resource. Instead, the request uses a temporary token with a limited validity period, which improves security significantly.

Testing with Postman

Postman can be used to test OAuth 2.0 authentication in multiple ways. Each method achieves the same goal, but the level of automation differs.

Manual Bearer Token

In the first method, the access token is generated manually by calling the authorization server. Once the token is returned, it is copied and added manually to the request header.

Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

 This method is useful for understanding the full authentication process step by step.

The returned access token is then used as the key to access the tenant.

As explained previously, in Basic Authentication the Authorization header contains the Base64-encoded client credentials. With OAuth 2.0, this changes: the Authorization header now uses a Bearer token instead of Basic Authentication credentials.

The first testing approach consists of sending a request to the HTTP endpoint. Note that this is the Cloud Integration resource endpoint (iFlow endpoint), not the authorization server used to retrieve the token. In this case, Postman’s built-in authorization type is not used. Instead, the generated access token is added manually in the request header as follows:

If the token is valid, the Cloud Integration endpoint returns the requested resource successfully.

Bearer Token in Postman

Postman also provides a built-in Bearer Token authorization type. In this case, the token is pasted into the Bearer Token field, and Postman automatically adds the Authorization header.

This method is simpler than manually editing the headers and is suitable when the token has already been generated.

Postman automatically adds an Authorization header containing the Bearer token that was pasted in the Bearer Token field. This is functionally the same as adding the header manually, but it is simpler and more convenient.

The Authorization header was added automatically by Postman.

OAuth 2.0 Configuration in Postman

The most efficient method is to configure OAuth 2.0 directly in Postman.

In this setup, the following information is provided:

  • token name,
  • grant type as Client Credentials,
  • client ID,
  • client secret,
  • token URL.

After clicking Get New Access Token, Postman sends the token request automatically. After clicking Use Token, the token is attached to the request and used directly.

Fill in the fields as shown in the screenshot below:

  • Access Token URL = tokenurl
  • Client ID = clientid
  • Client Secret = clientsecret

The client ID and client secret are the credentials generated in SAP BTP, which are normally used for Basic Authentication when requesting a token.

Then click Get New Access Token. This button performs automatically what was previously done manually: it sends a request to the authorization server using the provided credentials and retrieves the access token in a single step.

After clicking Use Token, the generated access token is automatically applied to the request.

As shown above, the Authorization header is automatically populated with the generated Bearer token.

The request can now be sent to the resource server, and if the token is valid, the expected result will be returned securely.

This is the most practical approach for repeated testing because it avoids generating and copying the token manually each time.

Best Practices and Security Considerations

When implementing OAuth 2.0 in SAP Cloud Integration, several best practices should be followed to ensure a secure and maintainable integration design.

Client credentials should never be exposed in shared documents, emails, or screenshots. Any sensitive values such as client ID, client secret, or access token should be masked before publishing the document internally.

OAuth 2.0 should be preferred over Basic Authentication whenever the integration requires secure access to protected resources. Tokens should be treated as temporary credentials and handled carefully, especially when testing in external tools such as Postman.

It is also important to consider token expiration and renewal during development and testing. A request may fail not because of configuration issues, but simply because the token has expired.

Following these practices helps ensure that the integration remains secure, professional, and aligned with enterprise standards.

Conclusion

OAuth 2.0 provides a more secure and scalable authentication mechanism for SAP Cloud Integration than Basic Authentication.

By using the Client Credentials flow, client credentials are exchanged once with the authorization server to obtain a temporary access token, which is then used to access protected Cloud Integration endpoints securely.

This document demonstrated the complete authentication process, from understanding the limitations of Basic Authentication to generating, applying, and testing OAuth 2.0 access tokens in Postman. It also showed how the authorization server and Cloud Integration resource endpoints interact within SAP BTP to support secure system-to-system communication.

By adopting OAuth 2.0 and following the recommended security practices, integrations can be implemented in a way that is more secure, maintainable, and aligned with enterprise standards.

entry.title: Updating Bank Accounts on Business Partner using SAP Service Layer on SAP Business One
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I’m trying to update the bank accounts of a Business Partner using SAP Service Layer. My JSON looks like this:

{
"CardCode": "P000454",
"BPBankAccounts": [
{
"AccountNo": "100005411",
"BankCode": "102301063",
"AccountName": "Robinson Grinding and Profiling, LLC JAS",
"Country": "US",
"InternalKey": 6999
}
]
}

When I perform a GET on the same Business Partner, the bank accounts segment returns this:

"BPBankAccounts": [
{
"LogInstance": 0,
"UserNo4": null,
"BPCode": "P000454",
"County": null,
"State": null,
"UserNo2": null,
"IBAN": "",
"ZipCode": null,
"City": null,
"Block": null,
"Branch": null,
"Country": "US",
"Street": null,
"ControlKey": "",
"UserNo3": null,
"BankCode": "102301063",
"AccountNo": "100005411",
"UserNo1": null,
"InternalKey": 6999,
"BuildingFloorRoom": null,
"BIK": null,
"AccountName": "Robinson Grinding and Profiling, LLC",
"CorrespondentAccount": null,
"Phone": null,
"Fax": null,
"CustomerIdNumber": "0",
"ISRBillerID": null,
"ISRType": 2,
"BICSwiftCode": "",
"ABARoutingNumber": null,
"MandateID": null,
"SignatureDate": null,
"MandateExpDate": null,
"SEPASeqType": null,
"U_SEI_LegalName": null,
"U_SEI_Moneda": null,
"U_SEI_TipoC": null,
"U_SEI_CCI": null,
"U_MSS_CtaDet": null
}
]

I included the "InternalKey" in my JSON to see if it helps identify the account, but I keep getting an error saying that the account already exists. How can I solve this issue?. If I use PATCH, it will include a new bank account. But if a use PUT, service layer will ask for the rest of the properties of the business partners which I don't intend to change. 

Many Thanks
SAP Business One, version for SAP HANA SAP Business One
